chunk_summary: Outpatient CMAM in India faces challenges with therapeutic food availability, requiring structured adaptations during stock-outs and standardized visit workflows for effective SAM management.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of therapeutic food in outpatient SAM management in India.
Learn structured adaptation protocols for therapeutic food stock-outs based on WHO guidelines.
Identify key components of a standardized outpatient CMAM visit workflow.
Recognize the importance of triage, follow-up, and household risk assessment in SAM care.
key_facts: India lacks a universally adopted therapeutic food for SAM; states use RUTF, locally produced equivalents, or state-designed supplements.
Inpatient care often relies on locally prepared F-75 and F-100 recipes as per MoHFW guidelines.
WHO 2023 framework allows dose reduction only after a child transitions out of SAM criteria and has no nutritional oedema.
Outpatient CMAM visits should include danger sign screening, appetite verification, weight measurement, and MUAC/WHZ tracking.
Stock-out adaptations must be predefined to ensure protocol reliability and avoid improvisation.
reference_quote: The 2023 WHO framework describes structured dose-reduction only after a child transitions out of SAM criteria and has no nutritional oedema, and only when complementary foods and follow-up are reliable.
